sary to preserve the health and COM
fort of the citizens of New York,
and to render the combination unable

resolution also makes a request of
President Roosevelt to instruct
United States Attorney General Knox
to proceed at once against the coal

bination dissolved in the interests of

1.-THE attempt to use the injunction
of a court" writes Rev. P. c. York
of San Francisco. ito deprive the
laboring man of trial by jury should
alarm all the people, for, while the
wage-earner is the first to feel it's
effects the principle which underlies
government by injunction is so far
reaching that no one can hope to
escape ultimately. The thing forbid
den by an injunction would without
the injunction be either legal or tile
gal. If it would be legal the judge
usurps the function of the legislature
when he forbids it. If it would be
Illegal. the injunction of the court
would be- unnecessary. for anyone
who violates the law can. upon con-
viction, be made to suffer the penal
ties presented. The meanest thief
and the most brutal murderer are
entitled to trial by jury; why should
this right be denied the laboring
man? Those who oppose government
by injunction are not in favor of
lawlessness. They are. on the con-
trary, the best friends of law and
order. They deny the right of any
